slides.title: Programs That Talk Back
slides.body: Every useful program needs to **show** information and **receive** information. In Python, `print()` and `input()` are the two tools that make your programs feel alive.
slides.tip: Think of print() as your program speaking and input() as your program listening.

slides.title: print() — Show Anything
slides.body: `print()` displays a value on screen and moves to the next line. Pass it text, numbers, or **multiple values separated by commas** — they all work.
slides.output:
Hello, world!
Your score: 95 out of 100
slides.tip: Python automatically puts a space between comma-separated values.

slides.title: sep and end: Fine-Tune Your Output
slides.body: Control what goes **between** values with `sep`, and what goes **at the end** of a line with `end`. Both have sensible defaults you can override.
slides.tip: end="" keeps the next print on the same line — great for progress dots.

slides.title: input() — Ask the User
slides.body: `input()` pauses your program, shows a **prompt**, and returns whatever the user types as a string. Always store it in a variable.
slides.code:
name = input("What is your name? ")
print("Nice to meet you,", name)
slides.output:
What is your name? Alice
Nice to meet you, Alice
slides.tip: Leave a trailing space inside your prompt string so the cursor isn't crammed against the colon.

slides.title: input() Always Returns a String
slides.body: Even if the user types `42`, Python hands you the **text** `"42"`. Wrap `input()` in `int()` or `float()` before doing any maths.
slides.code:
age = int(input("How old are you? "))
print(f"Next year you will be {age + 1}.")
slides.output:
How old are you? 20
Next year you will be 21.
slides.tip: f-strings let you embed variables with {} — much neater than comma-separated print().

slides.title: You Can Talk to Your Programs
slides.body: You now know how to **display output** with `print()`, **collect input** with `input()`, **convert** it to numbers, and format clean messages with **f-strings**. That's everything you need to build interactive programs.
slides.tip: The tip calculator exercise puts all four skills together — give it a go!
